coredata.dc:title: Antimicrobial activity and mechanisms of carbon quantum dot decorated modified zinc oxide nanoparticles against oral pathogenic bacteria
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yayu
Huang, Pengcheng
Wu, Yuqiong
Liu, Chaojin
coredata.dc:description: Caries represents one of the most prevalent bacterial infectious diseases in the oral cavity. Over decades, researchers have been actively seeking nanomaterials capable of inhibiting oral pathogenic bacteria. We utilized a one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize CQDs with multicolor luminescence and CQD-loaded ZnO composite nanomaterials. FE-SEM revealed that the rod-like structure of CQDs/ZnO becomes finer with increased CQDs content. XRD and XPS confirmed the presence of all components within the nanocomposites. We compared the antimicrobial activities of ZnO, 7.7% and 14.3% CQDs/ZnO against Streptococcus mutans, Enterococcus faecalis, Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus. The 7.7% CQDs/ZnO composite exhibited the most effective antibacterial activity. The 7.7% CQDs/ZnO composite exhibited the most effective antibacterial activity. FE-SEM and ONPG assays demonstrated that 7.7% CQDs/ZnO disrupted the membrane structure and alters membrane permeability. ESR, DPBF and ROS assayed further confirmed that 1O2 produced by 7.7% CQDs/ZnO accumulated at the cell membrane and disrupts its structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zinc oxide
Antibacterial

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of ZnO (a), 7.7% CQDs/ZnO (b) and 14.3% CQD/ZnO (c).
The XRD patterns (a), the FTIR spectra (b) and the average hydrodynamic size (c) of the samples, the CQDs solution exhibits fluorescence when exposed to UV 365 nm (d).
XPS fully scanned spectra of ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO (a); Zn 2p scan (b)；O 1 s scan (c); Zn 2p scan(d).

Bacterial viabilities of E. faecalis, S. mutans, E. coli and S. aureus treated with different samples (a). bacterial viabilities of E. faecalis, S. mutans, E. coli and S. aureus treated with different conditions of 7.7% CQDs/ZnO (b). The ONPG hydrolysis assay of E. faecalis, S. mutans, E. coli and S. aureus treated with different samples (c). SEM images of E. faecalis, S. mutans, E. coli and S. aureus treated with different samples (d).

Decompositon curve of DPBF by pure water, ZnO, 7.7% CQDs/ZnO, 14.3% CQDs/ZnO (a-d). The absorbance drop rate graph of pure water, ZnO, 7.7% CQDs/ZnO, 14.3% CQDs/ZnO (e). ESR signals of the DMPO-1O2 spin adducts for ZnO, 7.7% CQDs/ZnO, 14.3% CQDs/ZnO (f). The corresponding fluorescence microscopic images of relative ROS level (g) in E. faecalis, S. mutans, E. coli and S. aureus after different treatments.

head.ce:title: Antimicrobial activity and mechanisms of carbon quantum dot decorated modified zinc oxide nanoparticles against oral pathogenic bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Stomatology, the Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Quanzhou, Fujian, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Caries represents one of the most prevalent bacterial infectious diseases in the oral cavity. Over decades, researchers have been actively seeking nanomaterials capable of inhibiting oral pathogenic bacteria. We utilized a one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize CQDs with multicolor luminescence and CQD-loaded ZnO composite nanomaterials. FE-SEM revealed that the rod-like structure of CQDs/ZnO becomes finer with increased CQDs content. XRD and XPS confirmed the presence of all components within the nanocomposites. We compared the antimicrobial activities of ZnO, 7.7% and 14.3% CQDs/ZnO against Streptococcus mutans, Enterococcus faecalis, Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus. The 7.7% CQDs/ZnO composite exhibited the most effective antibacterial activity. The 7.7% CQDs/ZnO composite exhibited the most effective antibacterial activity. FE-SEM and ONPG assays demonstrated that 7.7% CQDs/ZnO disrupted the membrane structure and alters membrane permeability. ESR, DPBF and ROS assayed further confirmed that 1O2 produced by 7.7% CQDs/ZnO accumulated at the cell membrane and disrupts its structure.

ce:section.ce:para: The oral cavity harbors a diverse array of microorganisms, including bacteria, viruses, and fungi [1]. Dental caries, the most common infectious disease within the oral domain, results from intricate interactions among bacteria, high carbohydrate consumption, host susceptibility, and time, leading to demineralization of dental hard tissues [2,3]. Streptococcus mutans (S. mutans), a Gram-positive endogenous bacterium, stands as a primary cariogenic microorganism [4]. It is the most prevalent bacterium found in cariogenic plaques, producing short-chain organic acids that metabolize sucrose and induce the synthesis of extracellular polysaccharides, thereby promoting bacterial adhesion to the tooth surface and enhancing biofilm formation. Enterococcus faecalis (E. faecalis), a facultative anaerobic Gram-positive bacterium, primarily resides in teeth treated with root canal therapy. It possesses the capability to adhere to teeth in adverse environments and form biofilms [5].

In the 1980 s and 1990 s, antimicrobial drugs and antibiotics such as tetracycline and erythromycin were employed to combat S. mutans, aiming to minimize diseases induced by dental plaque biofilms, such as dental caries [6]. With the escalating misuse and overuse of antimicrobial drugs, bacterial resistance has gradually intensified [7]. Consequently, the development of novel antimicrobial materials to overcome antibiotic resistance and inhibit bacterial proliferation has become a pivotal research focus in the biomedical field [8].

Metal oxide powders have been employed in the formulation of restorative materials to enhance the mechanical strength of fillings, for a long time [9]. ZnO, as an opaque reinforcing filler incorporated into resin composite materials, can improve the physical properties of the resin, such as flexural modulus and compressive strength [10]. Moreover, ZnO possesses antibacterial properties [11]. The application of nanomaterials has garnered significant attention in the development of anticaries agents. Compared to traditional antibiotics, nanomaterials generally exhibit enhanced membrane permeability, cell-disrupting capabilities, and lower resistance [12].

ZnO NPs demonstrate superior performance in terms of physical, chemical, and mechanical properties compared to its larger micron-sized counterpart. Not only do ZnO NPs exhibit effective antibacterial action against cariogenic bacteria, but they also demonstrate significant anti-plaque effects [13]. Doped ZnO NPs can alter their physicochemical properties, such as particle size and surface area, playing a crucial role in enhancing antibacterial activity [14]. AgNP/ZnO has been shown to enhance the antibacterial activity of ZnO NPs against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) [15].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), emerging as a class of quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials with a diameter less than 10 nm, exhibit characteristics such as non-toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and water solubility [16]. In recent years, CQDs have garnered broader attention in antibacterial and related applications [17]. Functionalizing the surface of CQDs for the attachment of receptor-specific ligands can ultimately enhance drug efficacy and reduce side effects. This feature may be an ideal choice for effective chemical therapy, gene therapy, and antibiotic therapy [18,19]. The combination of CQDs with the dye PS can synergistically inhibit the growth of E. coli, and this inhibitory effect is associated with an increase in the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [20]. CQDs exhibit a high ROS yield, and their combination with other materials enhances antibacterial effects [21].

The synthesis methods for CQDs nanocomposites primarily fall into two categories: the “top-down” approach, which includes techniques such as laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical synthesis; and the “bottom-up” approach, which encompasses methods like microwave-assisted synthesis and hydrothermal synthesis. The hydrothermal method is particularly noteworthy for its simplicity and environmental friendliness, as it does not require toxic chemicals and often uses carbohydrates or natural polymer precursors as raw materials. This method is widely applied for the production of uniformly sized carbon dots and enhances resource utilization efficiency.

The aim of this study is to synthesize CQDs and the CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite using a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method, and to characterize their properties. ZnO doped with 7.7 % CQDs exhibited significantly enhanced antibacterial effects without the need for light assistance. The impact of ZnO and CQDs/ZnO on cell membranes was investigated using FE-SEM and ONPG assays. Further insights into the mechanisms of membrane disruption by ZnO and CQDs/ZnO were obtained through ESR, DPBF, and ROS analyses. Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis and antibacterial mechanism of the CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite.

S. Mutans and E. faecalis were supplied by School and Hospital of Stomatology, Fujian Medical University. E. coli and S. aureus was supplied by Microbiology Laboratory, the Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University. Chitosan was purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation. Citric acid was purchased from Xilong Scientific CO., LTD. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide(CTAB) were purchased from Aladdin Scientific CO., LTD. 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was purchased from Medchemexpress. Dimethyl sulfoxide was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. O-nitrophenyl β-galactoside (ONPG) was purchased from Beyotime Biotech. Inc. Deionized (DI) water was used in all the experiments. All the chemicals used were of analytical grade.

CQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. 1 g of Chitosan and a 0.5 M citric acid solution were stirred at a low speed overnight until completely dissolved, yielding a homogeneous solution. The solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and processed at 180 °C for 800 min. After cooling to room temperature, a brownish-yellow solution was obtained and set aside for further use.

5.95 g of hexahydrated zinc nitrate was dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water under continuous stirring. After 10 min, 1.6 g of sodium hydroxide was added, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.5 g of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was added, and the solution was stirred for an additional 45 min. The resulting solution was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and processed at 90 °C for 16 h. The precipitate obtained after completing the hydrothermal reaction was centrifuged, washed with water, and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain a white solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of different concentrations of CQD/ZnO composite
ce:section.ce:para: 5.95 g of hexahydrated zinc nitrate was dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water under continuous stirring. After 10 min, 1.6 g of sodium hydroxide was added, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Following this, 0.5 g of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide was added, and the solution was stirred for an additional 45 min. Subsequently, 5 mL and 10 mL of CQDs were separately added to two portions of the brownish solution, and stirring continued for 45 min. The resulting solutions were then transferred to polytetrafluoroethylene reactors and processed at 90 °C for 16 h. The precipitates obtained after completing the hydrothermal reaction were centrifuged, washed with water, and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain light yellow solids (7.7 % CQD/ZnO and 14.3 % CQD/ZnO).

The morphology of the prepared ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO, and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO was characterized using high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL, JAPAN). Dymanic light scattering (DLS) was measured by zeta-sizer nanoanalyzer (Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK). X-ray diffraction (Rigaku Ultima IV, JAPAN) analysis was performed to determine lattice parameters and crystal phases, utilizing Cu-Kα radiation at 40 kV and 30 mA. Surface elements and their states were detected through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi multitechnique) using a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier-transform infrared ATR-FTIR (Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet iS20, USA) was used to verify the presence of organic compounds. The photoluminescence (PL) study was carried out with a spectrophotometer (FLS980, Edinburgh, UK).

The bactericidal efficacy of ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO, and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO against S. mutans, E. faecalis, E. coli and S. aureus was assessed using the plate dilution method under different conditions. The bacterial concentration was adjusted to 1.0 × 106 CFU/mL. The study was divided into control and experimental groups. In the experimental groups, different concentrations of ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO, and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO were incubated with S. mutans, E. faecalis, E. coli and S. aureus for 30 min. The antibacterial rate was calculated using the following equation: Bactericidal rate %=
A - B
A
× 100 %
(1).
where A represents the control group and B represents the experimental group.

SEM was used to closely observe the membrane morphology of S. mutans, E. faecalis, E. coli and S. aureus. Firstly, replace the LB medium in the sample with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde. S. mutans, E. faecalis, E. coli and S. aureus were immobilized for 2 h, then the samples were rinsed with sterilized phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH=7.4) at least 3 times. Secondly, the samples were dehydrated with alcohol (10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 %) for 15 min. Finaly, dry the samples in a 4℃ refrigerator.

The effect of the samples on the membrane permeability of S. mutans, E. faecalis, E. coli and S. aureus was evaluated using ONPG. The bacterial suspensions were mixed with an isopropyl β-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG) solution (10 μg/mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. The suspensions were then centrifuged at low temperature for 5 min at 5000 rpm. The supernatant was discarded and the bacteria were resuspended in PBS and adjusted to an OD600 of 0.05–0.1 for the antibacterial experiments. Finally, 15 μL of the bacterial suspension, 15 μL of ONPG solution (3.7 mg/mL), 10 μL of 7 % DMSO, and 110 μL of PBS were mixed thoroughly. The absorbance at 420 nm (OD420) was measured.

We utilized a ROS assay kit (Baiaolaibo Bioengineering Institute Co. LTD., Beijing, China) to assess the levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) within bacterial cells. A stepwise approach was employed: bacteria were judiciously collected via refrigerated centrifugation (6000 rpm, 5 min). Subsequently, the bacterially harbored DCFH-DA was introduced into the mix, fostering a 30-minute co-culturing period within the ambient confines of a 37℃ incubator, ensconced in absolute darkness. Co-cultured DCFH-DA underwent meticulous removal via centrifugation. Visual confirmation of ROS expression was then achieved through fluorescence microscopy (DMI8, Leica, Germany).

DPBF serves as a fluorescence probe indicative of 1O2 levels. DPBF exhibits high specificity for 1O2, with its absorbance at 420 nm decreasing as a result of intensified reaction with 1O2. Therefore, the 1O2 levels in the solution system were determined based on the absorbance of DPBF. DPBF was weighed under subdued light and dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to prepare a 10 µg/mL solution. 0.5 g of ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO, and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO were individually mixed with 1 mL of DPBF solution, and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. The absorbance at 420 nm was measured using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader at 0, 10, 20, and 30 min.

To determine the redox capability of the samples, Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) measurements were conducted to confirm the ROS formation. In detail, 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was used as 1O2 trapping agent. 1 mg/mL ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO and 5 µL of trapping agent were irradiated by the NIR (808 nm). ESR spectra were recorded to analyse the 1O2.

SEM images revealed that pure ZnO exhibited a rod-like or columnar structure (Fig. 1 a). Upon modification with CQDs, the ZnO surface showed significant morphological changes. The nanorods in the 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO composites became more elongated (Fig. 1b), while an increase in CQD content to 14.3 % resulted in nanorods that appeared needle-like (Fig. 1c).

As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks of ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO, and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO exhibit similar peak intensities, presenting nine characteristic diffraction peaks. The positions of these peaks corresponded to the hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO, consistent with the peak data from the hexagonal wurtzite standard card (JCPDS NO.36–1451). Specifically, the peaks corresponded to ZnO's (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201) crystal planes. This indicated that the prepared samples exhibited the standard hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO. As shown in Fig. 2c, the average hydrodynamic size of the nanocomposites is 615, 955, 1720 nm for ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO. The corresponding EDX results (Fig. S1a) demonstrated the uniform distribution of Zn, O, and C in 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO in 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO, confirming the successful preparation of 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO.

The FTIR spectra of ZnO and CQDs/ZnO nanocomposites were analyzed, highlighting changes in absorption peaks and stretching vibrations. The results of the FTIR analysis of the unmoderate and CQDs-moderated ZnO nanoparticles are presented in Fig. 1b. All samples exbibited similar FTIR spectra, with characteristic vibrational bands in the 3392, 1634, 1384 cm−1, which correspond to –OH (stretching mode), C=O (stretching vibration), O-C-O (stretching vibration), respectively [22]. We tentatively assign the resonance at 800 cm-1 to the stretching of ZnO4. A slight shift in the position and intensity of the peak was observed after moderating [23].

Intriguingly, under the influence of ultraviolet light, particularly UV at 365 nm, the CQDs' electrons engross themselves in an energetic dance, thus catapulting into an excited state. The very wavelength of the light absorbed was judiciously determined by the chasm of energy between the ground state and the said exalted state. Consequently, the ethereal expanse of the CQDs solution basked in luminescence when accosted by the caressing touch of UV at 365 nm, a remarkable manifestation captured artfully within Fig. 1d.

The pronounced PL peak indicates strong visible light emission, attributed to radiative recombination between different metastable defect states. As shown in Fig. S1b, the intensity of the DLE peak decreased with CQDs loading of 7.7 %, suggesting a reduced electron-hole recombination rate post-loading. However, at CQDs loading of 14.3 %, the peak intensity increased, indicating an enhanced recombination rate [24]. It could be due to the elimination of defect states or an increase in defect states acting as recombination centers upon moderating.

TEM analysis was conducted to further confirm the morphological structure of 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO (Fig. 3 ). In the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, clear lattice fringes were observable, and the measured fringe spacing was 0.26 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of standard hexagonal wurtzite ZnO. Additionally, HRTEM revealed lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs, indicating the successful loading of CQDs onto ZnO.

Additionally, the composition and surface properties of ZnO and the CQDs/ZnO composite were investigated through XPS. The comprehensive survey spectrum in Fig. 4 indicated that no additional peaks corresponding to elements other than carbon (C 1 s), zinc (Zn 2p), and oxygen (O 1 s) were observed in the CQDs/ZnO composite. The high-resolution XPS spectrum in the C 1 s region around 285 eV was shown in Fig. 4d. The peak at 284.6 eV was attributed to extensively delocalized alternating carbon-hydrogen compounds, and the peak at 286.5 eV was characteristic of the C-C bond combination. Furthermore, the peak at 288.7 eV corresponded to carboxyl carbon (O-C=O). Each O 1 s XPS appeared was broad and asymmetric, indicating the presence of more than one chemical state (Fig. 4c). The lower binding energy component at 530.3 eV was attributed to hexagonal Zn 2p ions in the wurtzite structure of metal oxide (Fig. 4b). Peaks at 531.6 and 532.9 eV were associated with surface hydroxyl groups (OH) and oxygen forming single-bonded carbon (CO), respectively. The Zn 2p spectrum displayed two symmetric peaks, with the peak centered at 1021.6 eV corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and another centered at 1044.6 eV assigned to Zn 2p1/2, indicating that the Zn 2p in the CQDs/ZnO heterojunction was in a normal state. These results further confirmed the heterojunction composed of ZnO and CQDs.

The antibacterial experiment evaluated the antimicrobial properties of the samples against S. mutans, E. faecalis, E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 5 a and S2). After treatment with the samples at a concentration of 0.25 mg/mL for 30 min, the antibacterial efficiency was observed as follows:
− Against E. faecali: ZnO (40 %), 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO (92 %), 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO (82 %).
− Against S. mutants: ZnO (49 %), 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO (92 %), 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO (81 %).
− Against E. coli: ZnO (86 %), 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO (100 %), 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO (100 %).
− Against S. aureus: ZnO (42 %), 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO (100 %), 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO (100 %).

The antibacterial efficiency of 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO exhibited dose dependence (Fig. 5b). With increasing concentrations of 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO (0.125, 0.25, 0.5 mg/mL), a significant reduction in bacterial colony numbers was observed. At a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, a 100 % sterilization effect was achieved against the four bacteria.

Further investigation of the antibacterial behavior was conducted using field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to study the morphology and microstructure of E. faecalis, S. mutans, S. aureus and E. coli (Fig. 5d). The bacteria in the control group maintained regular morphology and intact structure. Bacterial treated with ZnO exhibited slight wrinkling on the cell surface. Notably, bacteria treated with 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO displayed significant wrinkling and deformation. Bacteria treated with 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO exhibited pronounced wrinkling and deformation, along with noticeable pits, suggesting the leakage and degradation of cellular components, ultimately leading to cell death. It indicated that 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO possess excellent antibacterial properties. The impact of the samples on cell membrane permeability was studied using the ONPG hydrolysis assay [25]. Typically, more severe bacterial membrane damage facilitates the penetration of ONPG into the bacterial cells, where it reacts with intracellular β-D-galactosidase (β-Gal) to produce yellow o-nitrophenol, exhibiting a strong absorption peak at 405 nm. As shown in the figure, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO demonstrated the highest ONPG hydrolysis capability, indicating its substantial potential to disrupt bacterial membranes. These findings are consistent with the SEM morphological observations. As shown in the Fig. 5c, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO exhibited the highest ONPG hydrolysis capability, indicating that 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO can enhance the membrane permeability of bacteria.

The ability of the samples to quench 1O2 was measured using DPBF as a singlet oxygen probe (Fig. 6 a-e) [26,27]. DPBF, serving as a 1O2 scavenger [28,29], reacts rapidly with 1O2. 1O2 aggregates at the site of bacterial infection, disrupting the membrane cell structure, resulting in bactericidal effects [30]. The change in absorbance of DPBF at 420 nm reflects the relative amount of 1O2 in the solution.Control group absorbance remained nearly unchanged over time, while ZnO, 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO, and 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO groups exhibited significant decreases, indicating 1O2 consumption and generation. 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO showed the highest rate of decrease, suggesting its superior 1O2 generation capacity. The results of ESR testing clearly indicated that the typical signal of the TEM- compound in the 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO group was the strongest (Fig. 6f), followed by the 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO group and the ZnO group. This result further confirms that 1O2 is the main active substance in the antibacterial mechanism.

DCFH-DA was used to detect changes in fluorescence intensity to evaluate the level of ROS in bacteria. DCFH-DA is non-fluorescent and can freely pass through the cell membrane. Inside the cell, ROS can oxidize non-fluorescent DCFH to produce fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). The green fluorescence intensity is proportional to the ROS level. As shown in the Fig. 6g, no green fluorescence was observed in the control group. The 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO group exhibited the strongest fluorescence, followed by the 14.3 % CQDs/ZnO group and the ZnO group, indicating that 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO generated more ROS. These studies suggested that the bactericidal activity of 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO may be attributed to the production of ROS. ROS can kill pathogens by causing damage to the cell membrane and/or DNA [31].

In this study, CQDs/ZnO composite materials were successfully prepared through a simple hydrothermal method. Contrary to expectations, the antibacterial activity of CQDs/ZnO composite materials did not increase with the addition of CQDs. The bactericidal concentration of 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO against S. mutans, E. faecalis, and S. aureus was 0.5 mg/mL, while it was 0.25 mg/mL against E. coli.

Further investigations through ESR, DPBF, and DCFH-DA detection revealed that the antibacterial mechanism of 7.7 % CQDs/ZnO was related to the generation of ROS. ROS, known for their strong oxidative properties, can disrupt microbial structures, achieving bactericidal effects [32]. These results provide new insights for the development of novel antibiotics and offer potential candidate materials for the future treatment of infectious diseases caused by bacteria.
During the preparation of this work the author used chatGPT in order to improve readability and language.

Yayu Chen: Supervision, Software, Writing – original draft. Pengcheng Huang: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yuqiong Wu: Software, Resources, Project administration. Chaojin Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is jointly supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2022 J01791) and Fujian Medical University Sailing Project under Grant 2021QH1110.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rechem.2024.101655.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Candida Interactions with the Oral Bacterial Microbiota
J Fungi (basel)
